does: This paper identifies and analyzes the narration gap in hybrid language model-solver pipelines, specifically how formal solver outputs are converted back into natural language for users. The authors model LLM-solver loops as verified decision procedures and conduct empirical evaluation of five open-source models under adversarial prompt injection attacks. They demonstrate that while certificate gating preserves the solver verdict's soundness, adversaries can manipulate the natural language narration to invert verified conclusions across different phrasings and communication channels. The study explores hardened prompts as a mitigation strategy that reduces injection attacks but reveals fundamental tensions between robustness and usability in narration.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research integrating formal verification tools like SAT and SMT solvers into language model reasoning pipelines, extending prior studies that focused on formalization and decision phases. It responds to the broader literature on chain-of-thought prompting and its limitations, alongside emerging research on adversarial robustness in LLMs and prompt injection vulnerabilities. The paper connects to formal verification traditions where soundness guarantees are paramount, and to recent work examining the fragility of human-LLM interaction loops where intermediate formal results must be communicated back to users. It leverages insights from certified reasoning and semantic security in cryptography to frame the narration problem.
enables: This work opens directions for developing formally certified narration mechanisms that preserve the soundness guarantees established by solvers through to user-facing outputs. It points toward hardened narrative generation methods that resist adversarial perturbations while maintaining interpretability, potentially combining techniques from adversarial training, formal language specifications, and semantic-preserving transformations. The framework could enable safer deployment of LLM-solver hybrids in security-critical applications by establishing verifiable bounds on what can be claimed from formal proofs. Future work might explore end-to-end verification of entire pipelines, investigate provably robust narration strategies, and develop tools for auditing the fidelity of natural language descriptions of formal results.
